content:
# Exodus Chapter 31 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~52r–52v (PDF pp. 104–105); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 74–75 (LXX) | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_031_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_031_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_031_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_031_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

> **MANUSCRIPT OBSERVATION — THE SPIRIT AND THE SABBATH:** The Leningrad Codex presents Exodus 31 across two folio pages in standard prose column format with full Masoretic vocalization and cantillation. This chapter divides into three sections: the appointment of Bezalel and Oholiab as master craftsmen (vv. 1-11), the Sabbath commandment as covenant sign (vv. 12-17), and the giving of the stone tablets (v. 18). The chapter bridges the divine instructions (chs. 25-30) and their execution (chs. 35-40) by naming the human hands that will do the work — hands filled with ruakh Elohim ("the Spirit of God") — and then establishing the limit of that work: the Sabbath. The chapter closes with the single most concrete object in the entire Sinai narrative: two tablets of stone, written by the finger of God. The Vaticanus manuscript (pp. 74-75) follows the MT sequence closely throughout.

---

## The Appointment of Bezalel (vv. 1–5)

**1** And YHWH [va-yedabber YHWH] spoke [el-Mosheh] to Moses, saying [le'mor]:

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה לֵּאמֹר. **Key terms:** וַיְדַבֵּר (va-yedabber) = "and he spoke" — the standard divine speech formula, continuing directly from the instructions of chapter 30.

**2** See [re'eh], I have called [qarati] by name [ve-shem] Bezalel [Betsalel] son of [ben] Uri [Uri], son of [ben] Hur [Khur], of the tribe [le-matteh] of Judah [Yehudah].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads רְאֵה קָרָאתִי בְשֵׁם בְּצַלְאֵל בֶּן־אוּרִי בֶן־חוּר לְמַטֵּה יְהוּדָה. **Key terms:** קָרָאתִי בְשֵׁם (qarati ve-shem) = "I have called by name" — YHWH himself selects the craftsman; the calling is personal and specific. בְּצַלְאֵל (Betsalel) = "in the shadow of God" — from בְּצֵל (be-tsel, "in the shadow of") + אֵל (El, "God"); the master builder works under divine protection and presence.

**3** And I have filled [va-amalle] him [oto] with the Spirit [ruakh] of God [Elohim], in wisdom [be-khokhmah] and in understanding [u-vi-tevunah] and in knowledge [u-ve-da'at] and in all [u-ve-khol] work [melakhah],

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וָאֲמַלֵּא אֹתוֹ רוּחַ אֱלֹהִים בְּחָכְמָה וּבִתְבוּנָה וּבְדַעַת וּבְכָל־מְלָאכָה. **Key terms:** רוּחַ אֱלֹהִים (ruakh Elohim) = "Spirit of God" — the same phrase as Genesis 1:2, where the ruakh Elohim hovered over the waters before creation; now the Spirit that made the cosmos fills a human craftsman to build YHWH's dwelling. The three terms — חָכְמָה (khokhmah, "wisdom"), תְּבוּנָה (tevunah, "understanding"), דַּעַת (da'at, "knowledge") — form a comprehensive triad of intellectual and practical capacity, the same triad by which YHWH founded the earth (Prov 3:19-20).

**4** To devise [la-khashov] designs [makhashavot], to work [la'asot] in gold [ba-zahav] and in silver [u-va-kesef] and in bronze [u-va-nekhoshet],

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads לַחְשֹׁב מַחֲשָׁבֹת לַעֲשׂוֹת בַּזָּהָב וּבַכֶּסֶף וּבַנְּחֹשֶׁת. **Key terms:** לַחְשֹׁב מַחֲשָׁבֹת (la-khashov makhashavot) = "to devise designs, to think thoughts" — from חשׁב (khashav, "to think, plan, devise"); the craftsman's skill begins in the mind; the Spirit fills the imagination before the hands.

**5** And in cutting [u-va-kharoshet] of stone [even] for setting [le-malle], and in cutting [u-va-kharoshet] of wood [ets], to work [la'asot] in all [be-khol] work [melakhah].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וּבַחֲרֹשֶׁת אֶבֶן לְמַלֹּאת וּבַחֲרֹשֶׁת עֵץ לַעֲשׂוֹת בְּכָל־מְלָאכָה. **Key terms:** חֲרֹשֶׁת (kharoshet) = "cutting, craftsmanship" — from חרשׁ (kharash, "to cut, engrave, plow"); the term covers both stone-cutting and wood-carving. לְמַלֹּאת (le-malle) = "for setting, for filling" — the setting of gemstones into their mountings, as in the priestly breastpiece (28:17-20).

---

## The Appointment of Oholiab (vv. 6–11)

**6** And I [va-ani] — behold [hinneh], I have given [natatti] with him [itto] Oholiab [et-Oholiav] son of [ben] Ahisamach [Akhisamakh], of the tribe [le-matteh] of Dan [Dan]; and in the heart [u-ve-lev] of all [kol] the wise [khakham] of heart [lev] I have put [natatti] wisdom [khokhmah]; and they shall make [ve-asu] all [et-kol] that [asher] I have commanded you [tsivvitikha]:

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַאֲנִי הִנֵּה נָתַתִּי אִתּוֹ אֵת אָהֳלִיאָב בֶּן־אֲחִיסָמָךְ לְמַטֵּה־דָן וּבְלֵב כָּל־חֲכַם־לֵב נָתַתִּי חָכְמָה וְעָשׂוּ אֵת כָּל־אֲשֶׁר צִוִּיתִךָ. **Key terms:** אָהֳלִיאָב (Oholiav) = "my father's tent" or "tent of the father" — from אֹהֶל (ohel, "tent") + אָב (av, "father"); the assistant builder bears a name that evokes the very structure he will build. The pairing of Judah (Bezalel) and Dan (Oholiab) links the leading tribe and a lesser tribe in shared sacred work. וּבְלֵב כָּל־חֲכַם־לֵב נָתַתִּי חָכְמָה (u-ve-lev kol khakham-lev natatti khokhmah) = "and in the heart of all the wise of heart I have put wisdom" — YHWH gives skill to those already skilled; divine gifting and human capacity cooperate.

**7** The tent [et-ohel] of meeting [mo'ed], and the ark [ve-et-ha-aron] for the testimony [la-edut], and the mercy seat [ve-et-ha-kapporet] which [asher] is upon it [alav], and all [ve-et kol] the vessels [kelei] of the tent [ha-ohel],

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads אֵת אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד וְאֶת־הָאָרֹן לָעֵדֻת וְאֶת־הַכַּפֹּרֶת אֲשֶׁר עָלָיו וְאֵת כָּל כְּלֵי הָאֹהֶל. **Key terms:** לָעֵדֻת (la-edut) = "for the testimony" — the ark's defining purpose is to house the tablets of testimony; it is named by its contents.

**8** And the table [ve-et-ha-shulkhan] and its vessels [ve-et-kelav], and the pure [ha-tehorah] lampstand [ha-menorah] and all [ve-et-kol] its vessels [keleiha], and the altar [ve-et mizbeakh] of incense [ha-qetoret],

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְאֶת־הַשֻּׁלְחָן וְאֶת־כֵּלָיו וְאֶת־הַמְּנֹרָה הַטְּהֹרָה וְאֶת־כָּל־כֵּלֶיהָ וְאֵת מִזְבַּח הַקְּטֹרֶת. **Key terms:** הַטְּהֹרָה (ha-tehorah) = "the pure" — modifying the lampstand; this adjective (from טהר, taher, "to be clean, pure") refers to the pure gold from which the menorah is made (25:31); the LXX renders λυχνίαν τὴν καθαράν.

**9** And the altar [ve-et mizbeakh] of the burnt offering [ha-olah] and all [ve-et-kol] its vessels [kelav], and the basin [ve-et-ha-kiyyor] and its stand [ve-et-kanno],

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְאֶת מִזְבַּח הָעֹלָה וְאֶת־כָּל־כֵּלָיו וְאֶת־הַכִּיֹּר וְאֶת־כַּנּוֹ. **Key terms:** This inventory moves outward from the inner sanctuary (ark, mercy seat) through the holy place (table, lampstand, incense altar) to the courtyard (burnt offering altar, basin) — a comprehensive list of every item commanded in chapters 25-30.

**10** And the garments [ve-et bigdei] of the woven work [ha-serad], and the holy [ha-qodesh] garments [bigdei] for Aaron [le-Aharon] the priest [ha-kohen], and the garments [ve-et-bigdei] of his sons [banav], to be priests [le-khahen],

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְאֶת בִּגְדֵי הַשְּׂרָד וְאֶת־בִּגְדֵי הַקֹּדֶשׁ לְאַהֲרֹן הַכֹּהֵן וְאֶת־בִּגְדֵי בָנָיו לְכַהֵן. **Key terms:** בִּגְדֵי הַשְּׂרָד (bigdei ha-serad) = "garments of the woven work" or "garments of service" — the meaning of שְׂרָד (serad) is debated; it appears only in Exodus (31:10; 35:19; 39:1, 41) and likely refers to finely woven fabric or to garments specifically for service in the sanctuary. The LXX reads στολὰς τὰς λειτουργικάς ("liturgical robes").

**11** And the oil [ve-et shemen] of the anointing [ha-mishkhah], and the incense [ve-et-qetoret] of spices [ha-sammim] for the holy place [la-qodesh]; according to all [ke-khol] that [asher] I have commanded you [tsivvitikha] they shall do [ya'asu].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְאֶת שֶׁמֶן הַמִּשְׁחָה וְאֶת־קְטֹרֶת הַסַּמִּים לַקֹּדֶשׁ כְּכֹל אֲשֶׁר־צִוִּיתִךָ יַעֲשׂוּ. **Key terms:** כְּכֹל אֲשֶׁר־צִוִּיתִךָ יַעֲשׂוּ (ke-khol asher-tsivvitikha ya'asu) = "according to all that I have commanded you they shall do" — the concluding formula summarizes the entire inventory; the craftsmen's mandate is exact replication of the divine blueprint, not creative innovation.

---

## The Sabbath as Covenant Sign (vv. 12–17)

**12** And YHWH [va-yomer YHWH] said [el-Mosheh] to Moses, saying [le'mor]:

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה לֵּאמֹר. **Key terms:** וַיֹּאמֶר (va-yomer) = "and he said" — a shift from וַיְדַבֵּר (va-yedabber) of v. 1; the Sabbath command is introduced with the more intimate speech formula, marking a transition from technical instruction to covenantal declaration.

**13** And you [ve-attah] — speak [dabber] to [el] the sons of Israel [benei Yisrael], saying [le'mor]: Surely [akh] my Sabbaths [et-shabbtotai] you shall keep [tishmoru]; for [ki] it [hi] is a sign [ot] between me [beini] and between you [u-veineikhem] throughout your generations [le-doroteikhem], to know [la-da'at] that [ki] I [ani] am YHWH [YHWH] who sanctifies you [meqaddishkhem].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְאַתָּה דַּבֵּר אֶל־בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל לֵאמֹר אַךְ אֶת־שַׁבְּתֹתַי תִּשְׁמֹרוּ כִּי אוֹת הִוא בֵּינִי וּבֵינֵיכֶם לְדֹרֹתֵיכֶם לָדַעַת כִּי אֲנִי יְהוָה מְקַדִּשְׁכֶם. **Key terms:** אַךְ (akh) = "surely, nevertheless, only" — a restrictive particle; after eleven verses commanding work on the tabernacle, akh introduces the boundary: even sacred construction must yield to Sabbath rest. אוֹת (ot) = "sign" — the Sabbath is not merely a day but a sign (like the rainbow in Gen 9:12-13), a visible marker of the covenant relationship. מְקַדִּשְׁכֶם (meqaddishkhem) = "who sanctifies you" — piel participle of קדשׁ (qadash); YHWH is the active agent of Israel's holiness; the Sabbath is the evidence of that sanctification.

**14** And you shall keep [u-shmartem] the Sabbath [et-ha-shabbat], for [ki] holy [qodesh] it [hi] is to you [lakhem]; the one profaning it [mekhaleliehah] shall surely be put to death [mot yumat]; for [ki] everyone [kol] doing [ha-oseh] work [melakhah] on it [bah] — that soul [ha-nefesh ha-hi] shall be cut off [ve-nikhretah] from the midst [mi-qerev] of her people [ammeiha].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וּשְׁמַרְתֶּם אֶת־הַשַּׁבָּת כִּי קֹדֶשׁ הִוא לָכֶם מְחַלְלֶיהָ מוֹת יוּמָת כִּי כָּל־הָעֹשֶׂה בָהּ מְלָאכָה וְנִכְרְתָה הַנֶּפֶשׁ הַהִוא מִקֶּרֶב עַמֶּיהָ. **Key terms:** מוֹת יוּמָת (mot yumat) = "dying he shall die" or "he shall surely be put to death" — the infinitive absolute (mot) intensifies the finite verb (yumat); this is the strongest death-penalty formula in the Torah. The double penalty — execution and karet — underscores the absolute severity of Sabbath violation. מְחַלְלֶיהָ (mekhaleliehah) = "the one profaning it" — piel participle of חלל (khalal, "to profane, defile"); Sabbath-breaking is not mere disobedience but desecration.

**15** Six [sheshet] days [yamim] work [melakhah] shall be done [ye'aseh], and on the day [u-va-yom] the seventh [ha-shevi'i] — a Sabbath [shabbat] of complete rest [shabbaton], holy [qodesh] to YHWH [la-YHWH]; everyone [kol] doing [ha-oseh] work [melakhah] on the day [be-yom] of the Sabbath [ha-shabbat] shall surely be put to death [mot yumat].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads שֵׁשֶׁת יָמִים יֵעָשֶׂה מְלָאכָה וּבַיּוֹם הַשְּׁבִיעִי שַׁבַּת שַׁבָּתוֹן קֹדֶשׁ לַיהוָה כָּל־הָעֹשֶׂה מְלָאכָה בְּיוֹם הַשַּׁבָּת מוֹת יוּמָת. **Key terms:** שַׁבַּת שַׁבָּתוֹן (shabbat shabbaton) = "a Sabbath of complete rest" — the doubling intensifies the concept; shabbaton is an abstract noun of cessation, meaning the seventh day is not merely rest but the cessation of cessation, absolute stillness. יֵעָשֶׂה (ye'aseh) = niphal imperfect, "shall be done" — the passive voice distances the subject; the six days are for work, but even that work is described passively, while the Sabbath receives active, emphatic prohibition.

**16** And the sons of Israel [benei Yisrael] shall keep [ve-shameru] the Sabbath [et-ha-shabbat], to make [la'asot] the Sabbath [et-ha-shabbat] throughout their generations [le-dorotam] — a covenant [berit] of perpetuity [olam].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְשָׁמְרוּ בְנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵל אֶת־הַשַּׁבָּת לַעֲשׂוֹת אֶת־הַשַּׁבָּת לְדֹרֹתָם בְּרִית עוֹלָם. **Key terms:** בְּרִית עוֹלָם (berit olam) = "a covenant of perpetuity" — the Sabbath is not a regulation but a covenant, a binding relational agreement between YHWH and Israel that extends across all generations without expiration. לַעֲשׂוֹת אֶת־הַשַּׁבָּת (la'asot et-ha-shabbat) = "to make the Sabbath" — the verb עשׂה (asah, "to make, do") is striking; Israel does not merely observe the Sabbath but actively makes it, constructing rest as a positive act.

**17** Between me [beini] and between [u-vein] the sons of Israel [benei Yisrael] it [hi] is a sign [ot] for perpetuity [le-olam]; for [ki] six [sheshet] days [yamim] YHWH [YHWH] made [asah] the heavens [et-ha-shamayim] and the earth [ve-et-ha-arets], and on the day [u-va-yom] the seventh [ha-shevi'i] he ceased [shavat] and he refreshed himself [va-yinnafash].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads בֵּינִי וּבֵין בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל אוֹת הִוא לְעֹלָם כִּי־שֵׁשֶׁת יָמִים עָשָׂה יְהוָה אֶת־הַשָּׁמַיִם וְאֶת־הָאָרֶץ וּבַיּוֹם הַשְּׁבִיעִי שָׁבַת וַיִּנָּפַשׁ. **Key terms:** וַיִּנָּפַשׁ (va-yinnafash) = "and he refreshed himself" — niphal of נפשׁ (nafash), related to נֶפֶשׁ (nefesh, "soul, life, breath"); an extraordinary anthropomorphism — YHWH is depicted as catching his breath, being re-souled after the labor of creation. This verb appears only three times in the Hebrew Bible (Exod 23:12; 31:17; 2 Sam 16:14). The Sabbath grounds Israel's rest in the very rhythm of God's own creative activity. The LXX renders κατέπαυσεν ("he rested"), softening the anthropomorphism.

---

## The Tablets of Testimony (v. 18)

**18** And he gave [va-yitten] to [el] Moses [Mosheh], when he finished [ke-khalloto] speaking [le-dabber] with him [itto] on Mount [be-Har] Sinai [Sinai], the two [shnei] tablets [lukhot] of the testimony [ha-edut] — tablets [lukhot] of stone [even], written [ketuvim] by the finger [be-etsba'] of God [Elohim].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיִּתֵּן אֶל־מֹשֶׁה כְּכַלֹּתוֹ לְדַבֵּר אִתּוֹ בְּהַר סִינַי שְׁנֵי לֻחֹת הָעֵדֻת לֻחֹת אֶבֶן כְּתֻבִים בְּאֶצְבַּע אֱלֹהִים. **Key terms:** כְּכַלֹּתוֹ (ke-khalloto) = "when he finished" — piel infinitive construct of כלה (kalah, "to finish, complete") with temporal כ and 3ms suffix; the entire Sinai instruction (chs. 25-31) concludes at this moment. בְּאֶצְבַּע אֱלֹהִים (be-etsba' Elohim) = "by the finger of God" — the most physically concrete image of divine writing in the Hebrew Bible; the same phrase appears in Deut 9:10 and is echoed in the Egyptian magicians' recognition of the plagues (Exod 8:15 [Heb 8:19]). שְׁנֵי לֻחֹת הָעֵדֻת (shnei lukhot ha-edut) = "the two tablets of the testimony" — not "tablets of the law" but "tablets of the testimony/witness"; the tablets testify to the covenant relationship, not merely to legal obligation. The LXX renders δύο πλάκας τοῦ μαρτυρίου, πλάκας λιθίνας γεγραμμένας τῷ δακτύλῳ τοῦ θεοῦ ("two tablets of the testimony, stone tablets written by the finger of God").

---

## Translator's Afterword

Exodus 31 is the hinge between divine speech and human action — the chapter that names the builders and then immediately limits their building. After seven chapters of tabernacle blueprints (25-31), YHWH identifies who will execute the work, establishes when the work must stop, and delivers the covenant document into Moses' hands.

The appointment of Bezalel (vv. 1-5) introduces the first person in Scripture explicitly said to be filled with ruakh Elohim ("the Spirit of God") for a specific task. The three terms accompanying this filling — khokhmah ("wisdom"), tevunah ("understanding"), and da'at ("knowledge") — form the same triad that Proverbs 3:19-20 attributes to YHWH's own creative work: "YHWH by wisdom founded the earth; by understanding he established the heavens; by his knowledge the deeps broke open." The tabernacle builder receives the Creator's own capacities. This is no ordinary craftsmanship; it is participation in divine making. Bezalel's name — "in the shadow of God" — confirms the point: his work is sheltered by and derivative of the divine original.

Oholiab's appointment (v. 6) pairs the tribe of Judah with the tribe of Dan — the preeminent tribe and one of the humblest — in shared sacred labor. The statement that YHWH has placed wisdom "in the heart of all the wise of heart" (v. 6) reveals a theology of gifting: divine endowment does not replace human capacity but enhances what is already present. The inventory of items (vv. 7-11) then recapitulates every major element from chapters 25-30, creating a comprehensive checklist that the craftsmen must follow — ke-khol asher tsivvitikha ya'asu ("according to all that I have commanded you they shall do").

The Sabbath section (vv. 12-17) is placed with deliberate theological intent between the command to build and the actual building. The particle akh ("surely, nevertheless") in v. 13 signals the restriction: even the construction of YHWH's own dwelling must yield to the Sabbath. The Sabbath is called ot ("sign"), the same word used for the rainbow (Gen 9:12) and circumcision (Gen 17:11) — visible markers of covenant. It is called berit olam ("covenant of perpetuity") — not merely a law but a permanent relational bond. The death penalty (mot yumat, "he shall surely die") with its infinitive absolute intensification, repeated in both v. 14 and v. 15, places Sabbath violation on par with the most severe offenses in the Torah. The theological grounding in v. 17 reaches back to creation itself: YHWH shavat va-yinnafash ("ceased and refreshed himself"), using the extraordinary verb nafash — related to nefesh ("soul") — to depict God as re-souled by rest.

The final verse (v. 18) is one of the most physically vivid in all of Scripture. The two lukhot ha-edut ("tablets of the testimony") are stone, written be-etsba' Elohim ("by the finger of God"). After thirty-one chapters of spoken instruction, the narrative produces a tangible object — the only artifact in the Torah explicitly described as made by God's own hand. The phrase ke-khalloto le-dabber ("when he finished speaking") marks the formal close of the Sinai revelation begun in chapter 25. What follows (ch. 32) will shatter these tablets before Israel ever reads them.

Key MT-LXX divergences: (1) The Vaticanus text follows the MT closely throughout Exodus 31, with no significant additions or omissions. (2) The LXX renders ruakh Elohim (v. 3) as πνεῦμα θεῖον ("divine spirit"), using the adjective θεῖον rather than the genitive τοῦ θεοῦ ("of God"), a subtle but notable interpretive shift. (3) The LXX renders bigdei ha-serad (v. 10) as στολὰς τὰς λειτουργικάς ("liturgical robes"), interpreting the obscure serad as referring to service function rather than weaving technique. (4) The phrase va-yinnafash (v. 17) is rendered κατέπαυσεν ("he rested") in the LXX, eliminating the anthropomorphic "re-souling" of the Hebrew. (5) The LXX renders be-etsba' Elohim (v. 18) faithfully as τῷ δακτύλῳ τοῦ θεοῦ ("by the finger of God"), preserving the concrete anthropomorphism that v. 17's translation had softened. (6) As throughout, YHWH is rendered κύριος in the LXX.
